text: The surgeon inserts robotic arm ports for a left lower lobe sleeve resection, including a subxiphoid port. Using scissors, the surgeon pushes tissue over the pericardium to facilitate port placement.

The surgeon uses a lung grasper through a five-millimeter port to retract the lung forward, exposing the pulmonary artery and inferior pulmonary vein. The pleura is released posteriorly, and station seven lymph nodes are removed anterior to the vagus nerve. The inferior pulmonary ligament is visible, and the left main bronchus is identified above the pulmonary artery. The surgeon cleans the area, removing a station L-ten lymph node.

The surgeon displays the left main bronchus, lower lobe bronchus, upper lobe bronchus, and secondary carina. The view shifts anteriorly to reveal the superior pulmonary vein, noting the lingular vein's lower position than usual. Both the inferior and superior pulmonary veins are visible, with a gap between them. The surgeon begins transecting the inferior pulmonary vein using a stapler, followed by the pulmonary artery.

They biopsied this, and it came back as carcinoma in situ. This was biopsied a second time, which confirmed still carcinoma in situ, very close to the lower lobe take-off. We did further autofluorescence to confirm there was no carcinoma in situ in either the upper lobe or the left main bronchus. And then we planned to perform a sleeve lobectomy to get the maximum chance of full resection with clear margins.

The surgeon uses a TAMBODY-Five stapler to transect the basal branch of the pulmonary artery. The lung grasper is utilized to maneuver around the pulmonary artery due to the limited reach of the Progras. The surgeon efficiently staples all basal pulmonary artery branches, including the apical branch of the lower lobe, in a single firing. The lingular branch of the pulmonary artery is identified and preserved to avoid stenosis. The anterior fissure is also being addressed.

The surgeon cleans the left main bronchus, revealing the lower lobe bronchus inferiorly and the upper lobe bronchus left. Using curved scissors, an incision is made at the crux between the lower and upper lobes, right on the carina. The incision progresses through the secondary carina, opening up the bronchus. The left main bronchus is visible in the distance, illuminated by the bronchoscope, while the double lumen tube is also visible. The view extends into the lower lobe bronchus.

The surgeon adjusts the lower lobe to enhance visibility for completing the resection, creating a V-shaped incision in the lower lobe bronchus. The membranous part of the bronchus is cut, revealing the pulmonary artery above, the aorta to the right, and the superior vein with the lingular vein slightly lower than usual. The abnormality at the carina between the apical and basal segments of the lower lobe bronchus is visible.

The surgeon is fashioning the upper lobe bronchus, straightening it at an angle to improve resection margins. The upper lobe bronchus and the left main bronchus are prepared for anastomosis. The surgeon uses a Foro V-Lock suture, which features absorbable monofilament and one-way barbs for maintaining tension without needing a knot at the start.

The surgeon begins continuous suturing along the cartilaginous portion of the bronchus from superior to inferior using a V-lock suture, which maintains tension without loosening. The robotic system provides enhanced vision with eight times magnification, allowing precise manipulation. The suture cap needle driver is used in the right hand, with occasional use of scissors for cutting the suture.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing around the cartilaginous portion of the bronchus using a V-Lock suture, tightening it with each stitch. The suture remains taut due to its one-way design. The surgeon uses a large needle to take single bites, focusing on larger bites in the left main bronchus compared to the left upper lobe bronchus. The upper lobe vein is visible near the suture line, and the enhanced vision helps avoid accidental contact with the needle.

We've also performed a total lung-sparing sleeve using this exact same view, and it was very straightforward. And I've also watched Bob Sofoglio do a left-sided total lung-sparing sleeve with no problems at all.

The surgeon completes the last suture on the cartilaginous portion using a V-lock suture, ensuring it remains tight. The needle is then changed to begin suturing the membranous portion, starting from the upper border.

The surgeon uses the Prograsp instrument to handle the suture, demonstrating its strong grip that can snap the suture if not handled carefully. The Prograsp is visually similar to other instruments like the Cadillac or bipolar, but it has a much stronger grip, which can be hazardous in lung surgery as it may tear lung tissue.

The surgeon takes a new suture to repeat the initial row, performing continuous suturing along the membranous portion of the upper lobe bronchus. The suture is carefully passed through the delicate membranous tissue to avoid tearing, using the V-lock suture's eye for tension maintenance.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the thin membranous portion of the bronchus, ensuring not to tear it while using a curved needle to tighten the suture gently. The Progras instrument holds a thick part of the bronchus near the secondary carina, with the surgeon advising to cut just above this area to avoid dense tissue. The upper lobe is aligned with the left main bronchus, and the suture is tightened with a gentle pull.

The surgeon completes the final sutures using a V-lock suture on the bronchus. The Prograsp instrument is used to pull the suture, but its strong grip causes the suture to snap. A third V-lock is inserted, and the surgeon hand-ties a knot between the snapped suture and the new one, demonstrating the ease of robotic suturing.

I just don't think we've got the VATS instruments that have got the dexterity to suture in this way easily. And I'm not saying it's not possible. I'll just say this is where the power of the robot comes in in thoracic surgery. It's suturing because it was originally designed as a suturing device, and that's its real strength. Robotic lobectomy is excellent with stapled bronchial anastomosis, but really, when you have to suture, the robot becomes vastly superior to that. So I'm just making sure that they're not overlapped on the first suture.

The surgeon ties the final knot, connecting the cartilaginous suture to the membranous suture, using a needle driver. The knot is pushed down carefully to avoid snapping, as the robotic system provides visual feedback but lacks tactile feedback. The bronchoscopic view reveals the final anastomosis, which appears widely open, although the lingula is not fully inflated yet.

The surgeon performs an underwater check of the bronchial anastomosis, observing a few bubbles originating from the parenchyma. After adjusting the water level below the parenchyma, no bubbles are seen from the anastomosis. The sample is removed from the chest through a sub-xiphoid incision, which minimizes pain and rib spreading.

The surgeon checks the wide open margin of the lower lobe bronchus, ensuring a good margin. A drain is placed, and the lung is inflated. The video concludes with the final result showing five incisions and a sub-xiphoid removal.
